questions.question_text: A seller of a three-unit residential property in New York asks the listing agent to complete the Property Condition Disclosure Statement on the seller's behalf. How should the agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse because agents must not fill out the PCDS
distractors: Complete it as long as the seller reviews and signs off afterward
Fill it out but note the agent authored it
Offer the $500 credit option instead
questions.explanation: Under RPL Article 14, the Property Condition Disclosure Statement must be completed by the seller. An agent who fills it out risks liability and violates the statute. The $500 credit alternative was eliminated as of March 20, 2024, so the PCDS is now mandatory.

questions.question_text: Which residential properties are subject to New York's mandatory Property Condition Disclosure Statement?
questions.correct_answer: Residential properties of one to four units
distractors: All residential properties regardless of unit count
Only single-family detached homes
Properties with five or more dwelling units
questions.explanation: RPL Article 14 requires the PCDS for residential properties of one to four units. Larger multifamily properties, commercial properties, and certain exempt transactions such as estate sales are not covered by this requirement.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent is working with a client purchasing a home built in 1965. The seller provides the PCDS but makes no mention of lead paint. What additional disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: A separate federal lead paint disclosure
distractors: No additional disclosure when the PCDS is already complete
A state environmental impact assessment from the DEC
A county health department clearance letter
questions.explanation: Federal law requires a separate lead-based paint disclosure for all residential properties built before 1978. The PCDS does not satisfy this federal requirement, so sellers must provide the EPA-mandated lead paint disclosure and pamphlet independently.

questions.question_text: An executor is selling a deceased relative's home. The executor has never lived in the property. Must the executor provide a PCDS?
questions.correct_answer: No, estate sales are exempt from the PCDS requirement
distractors: Yes, all residential sales require a PCDS
Only if the property has four or more units
Yes, but the executor may use the $500 credit instead
questions.explanation: Estate sales are specifically exempt from the PCDS requirement under RPL Article 14. The rationale is that executors or administrators typically lack personal knowledge of the property's condition, making accurate disclosure impractical.

questions.question_text: Since March 20, 2024, what happens if a New York seller fails to deliver the PCDS to a buyer before signing the purchase contract?
questions.correct_answer: The seller is in violation of mandatory PCDS law
distractors: The buyer automatically receives a $500 credit at closing instead
The contract becomes voidable at the seller's option
The listing agent must complete the form instead
questions.explanation: The 2024 amendment to RPL Article 14 eliminated the $500 credit alternative. Sellers must now deliver a completed PCDS before the buyer signs the contract. Failure to comply exposes the seller to legal liability rather than triggering an automatic credit.

questions.question_text: Under New York Industrial Code Rule 56, what must be conducted before renovating or demolishing a building that may contain asbestos?
questions.correct_answer: An asbestos survey by a NYS DOL-certified inspector
distractors: A general home inspection by any licensed inspector
A Phase I environmental assessment by an engineer
A DEC air quality test of the surrounding area
questions.explanation: New York's Industrial Code Rule 56 (12 NYCRR Part 56), administered by the Department of Labor, requires an asbestos survey before any demolition, renovation, or remodeling of a building. The survey must be conducted by an inspector certified by the NYS DOL Asbestos Control Bureau.

questions.question_text: A buyer is considering a rural property and discovers an uncapped gas well on the parcel. Under which statute must this condition be disclosed?
distractors: Environmental Conservation Law Article 27
Real Property Law Article 14
General Business Law Section 349
questions.explanation: RPL Section 242 specifically addresses the disclosure of oil, gas, and solution mining activities, including uncapped wells. This statute requires sellers to disclose the presence and location of any wells on the property to prospective buyers.

questions.question_text: A listing agent knows the property has elevated radon levels from a previous test. New York has no state-mandated radon disclosure requirement. Must the agent disclose this information?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, known material facts must be disclosed regardless
distractors: No, because New York does not mandate radon disclosure
Only if the buyer requests radon test results
Only if the levels exceed EPA action guidelines
questions.explanation: Although New York does not have a specific radon disclosure statute, agents have a common-law duty to disclose all known material facts that could affect a buyer's decision. Elevated radon is a health hazard that constitutes a material fact requiring disclosure.

questions.question_text: Who is authorized to perform asbestos abatement work in New York?
questions.correct_answer: A contractor licensed by the NYS Department of Labor
distractors: Any general contractor with liability insurance
A DEC-certified environmental consultant
The property owner if the area is under 100 square feet
questions.explanation: Under Industrial Code Rule 56, only contractors licensed by the NYS Department of Labor may perform asbestos abatement. Workers must also hold individual DOL certifications. Training must come from a NYS Department of Health-accredited provider, and certifications require annual refresher courses.

questions.question_text: Underground storage tanks on New York properties are regulated by which state agency?
distractors: Department of Health
Department of State Division of Licensing
Office of General Services
questions.explanation: The New York Department of Environmental Conservation (DEC) regulates underground storage tanks, including registration, monitoring, and cleanup requirements. USTs can leak petroleum or chemicals into soil and groundwater, making them a significant environmental concern in real estate transactions.

questions.question_text: A developer purchases a contaminated former industrial site in New York and enrolls in the DEC Brownfield Cleanup Program. Upon completing cleanup, the developer receives a Certificate of Completion. What does this certificate provide?
questions.correct_answer: Liability relief and eligibility for state tax credits
distractors: Automatic rezoning to residential use
A waiver of all future environmental testing
Exemption from local property taxes for 10 years
questions.explanation: The NYS Brownfield Cleanup Program, administered by DEC under ECL Article 27, provides participants who complete cleanup with a Certificate of Completion that releases them from state environmental liability and makes them eligible for refundable tax credits covering site preparation and tangible property costs.

questions.question_text: A buyer discovers a leaking underground oil tank on a property they just purchased. Under NY Navigation Law Article 12, who is strictly liable for cleanup costs?
questions.correct_answer: The current property owner, regardless of fault
distractors: Only the person who originally installed the tank
The seller who owned the property when the leak began
The oil delivery company that last filled the tank
questions.explanation: NY Navigation Law Article 12 imposes strict liability for petroleum discharges on any party in a position to control the contamination, including the current property owner even if they did not cause the spill. This differs from CERCLA, which excludes petroleum from its definition of hazardous substances.

questions.question_text: Federal CERCLA excludes one major category of contamination from its definition of hazardous substances. Which New York state law fills this gap?
questions.correct_answer: Navigation Law Article 12 covering petroleum spills
distractors: Environmental Conservation Law Article 8 (SEQRA)
Real Property Law Article 14 (PCDA)
General Business Law Article 25-A
questions.explanation: CERCLA specifically excludes petroleum from its definition of hazardous substances. New York's Navigation Law Article 12 fills this gap by imposing strict liability for petroleum spills and discharges. The state also maintains an Oil Spill Fund to finance cleanup when responsible parties fail to act.

questions.question_text: Under NY DEC petroleum bulk storage regulations, who is responsible for registering underground and aboveground petroleum tanks that meet capacity thresholds?
distractors: The petroleum distributor that delivers fuel
The tank manufacturer or installer
The local fire department
questions.explanation: Under 6 NYCRR Part 613, the property owner is responsible for registering petroleum storage tanks with the DEC. Underground tanks of 110 gallons or more and facilities with combined aboveground capacity exceeding 1,100 gallons must be registered and renewed every five years.

questions.question_text: Since December 2022, the New York PCDS includes a new disclosure question addressing which specific environmental concern?
questions.correct_answer: Whether the property has been tested for indoor mold
distractors: Whether the property is within 500 feet of a cell tower
Whether the property has been tested for electromagnetic fields
Whether the property is in a FEMA flood zone
questions.explanation: In December 2022, New York amended the PCDS to add a question about indoor mold testing. This was the first explicit mold disclosure requirement on the form. Combined with the 2024 elimination of the $500 credit option, sellers must now address mold as part of the mandatory PCDS.
